# Plot: the amalgamation (1914)

## Taxonomy

[play_nigeria](play_nigeria.md)

## Owner

- Project: khai-cultures

## Cue

By the turn of the twentieth century the British, having pushed in along the coast and up the rivers, hold the southern and northern lands as two separate protectorates. In 1914, for their own convenience of rule and revenue, they decide to join the two into one administration and govern it as a single colony from the coast.

## Action

The northern and southern protectorates are amalgamated into one Nigeria, the name itself coined only a few years before. One border is drawn around the Yoruba and the Igbo and the Hausa-Fulani and the Edo and the Ijaw and all the rest, peoples who had never asked to share a state, ruled from [Lagos](place_lagos.md) under a system that mostly governed the north through its emirs and the south more directly, and that taught the colonizer's English as the one tongue the whole new country could share. A country is made on paper before it is made in the heart, and the long, central work of turning that line on a map into a people who feel themselves one begins.

## Stage

- [Lagos](place_lagos.md): the coastal city the British made the seat of the new colony, the door through which the country was ruled and the wider world came in.
- [the peoples](position_the_peoples.md): the many nations enclosed by one border, handed a shared country and a long argument over how to share it.

## Tension

The amalgamation gives Nigeria its shape and its hardest question in the same stroke: it binds many proud nations into one state without ever asking them, leaving the country both the gift of a vast, plural giant and the burden of a unity it must build for itself, since the line on the map was drawn for the ruler's ease and not for the ruled. Everything after is the people's own work of making that border mean a nation.
